{"success": true, "name": "ecommerce-codebase-audit", "description": "Audit, navigate, and iterate on a Next.js e-commerce project (admin panel + storefront). Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, deployment follow-through, and post-deployment client readiness review.", "tags": ["nextjs", "ecommerce", "admin", "audit", "tailwind", "layout", "sidebar", "supabase", "auth", "cloudflare", "org-restructuring", "package-extraction"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: ecommerce-codebase-audit description: "Audit, navigate, and iterate on a Next.js e-commerce project (admin panel + storefront). Covers layout patterns, theme system, visual asset inventory/cleanup, image gap analysis, auth cookie debugging, deployment follow-through, and post-deployment client readiness review." version: 1.5.0 tags: [nextjs, ecommerce, admin, audit, tailwind, layout, sidebar, supabase, auth, cloudflare, org-restructuring, package-extraction] --- # Ecommerce Codebase Audit Inspect and improve a Next.js e-commerce admin panel. Focus on layout consistency, theme application, page structure, image assets, and auth flow. ## Audit Checklist ### Layout Consistency - [ ] Every admin page wraps content in `AdminShell` from `@/components/admin/admin-layout` - [ ] `AdminShell` includes the sidebar navigation (responsive: hidden on mobile, fixed on desktop) - [ ] Sidebar uses the same links and active-state highlighting across all pages - [ ] All pages render inside `` not just individually ### Theme System The admin theme uses emerald/zinc on a dark background (#0a0a0b / #0f0f10): ```tsx // Tailwind classes to look for bg-[#0f0f10] // sidebar + card backgrounds bg-zinc-800/60 // borders text-emerald-400 // active text bg-emerald-500/10 // active background text-zinc-400 // inactive text hover:bg-zinc-800/40 // hover state ``` - [ ] All pages use the same color tokens (not hardcoded different colors) - [ ] Active page shows `bg-emerald-500/10 text-emerald-400` with indicator dot - [ ] Inactive pages use `text-zinc-400 hover:text-zinc-200 hover:bg-zinc-800/40` ### Page Structure Each admin page follows this pattern: ```tsx 'use client' import { AdminShell, useAdminAuth } from "@/components/admin/admin-layout" import { useState, useEffect } from "react" export default function PageName() { const { authed } = useAdminAuth() if (!authed) return null return ( <>

Page Title

{/* content */} ) } ``` - [ ] Pages use `{ authed } = useAdminAuth()` for auth guard - [ ] Pages use `AdminShell` wrapper (already in the root layout) - [ ] Pages use `fetch("/api/admin/*")` for data (not direct Supabase client) - [ ] Data fetched via `/api/admin/` routes that use service_role key server-side ### AdminShell Component Located in `@/components/admin/admin-layout` (or wherever it's imported from). The AdminShell provides: - Sidebar navigation with 4 groups: Operaciones, Catálogo, Marketing, Configuración - Mobile responsiveness (hidden sidebar on mobile, toggle with hamburger) - Auth guard via `useAdminAuth` - Consistent background and layout padding ## Auth Flow & Cookie Debugging ### The Core Trap: Two Auth Paths Crossing In Next.js + Supabase e-commerce projects, there are usually TWO auth paths that can conflict: **Path A — Consumer auth (useAuth):** - Login page calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()` directly from the browser client - Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` browser client sets the session cookie automatically - `/mi-cuenta` reads session via `supabase.auth.getSession()` — works because cookie exists **Path B — Admin auth (useAdminAuth / /api/auth):** - Login page calls `/api/auth` with `action: "login"` - API route creates an SSR client with `createServerClient()`, but often has `setAll() {}` empty - **The session cookie never gets set on the response** — the API returns tokens, but no cookie - Admin layout falls back to localStorage or custom cookie **Common failure modes:** 1. **Login works (gets tokens) but /mi-cuenta shows unauthenticated** — consumer login page fetches `/api/auth` instead of calling Supabase directly. Fix: use `useAuth().login()` (Supabase browser client) which sets the correct session cookie. 2. **Login works but /admin redirects back to /login** — admin layout's `useAdminAuth()` calls `/api/auth?action=me` with a token from localStorage, but the API route's token verification path fails. Fix: ensure the API route can verify tokens against Supabase REST API (not just SSR cookies). ### Fix Checklist - [ ] Consumer login uses Supabase browser client directly (`supabase.auth.signInWithPassword()`), NOT a custom `/api/auth` endpoint - [ ] After successful login, store session tokens in `localStorage` for admin layout fallback: `localStorage.setItem("elviajero_admin_session", JSON.stringify({ access_token, refresh_token }))` - [ ] Admin layout checks localStorage first, then Supabase SSR cookie, then custom cookie — triple fallback - [ ] API route `action: "me"` verifies tokens via `fetch(${supabaseUrl}/auth/v1/user)` not `supabase.auth.getUser()` - [ ] Service-role client for profile queries uses `createClient(url, serviceRoleKey)` — passing service key as second arg, not in headers - [ ] Role check queries DB profiles table (not JWT claim) — `profiles.role` not `user.role` ### Debugging Auth Issues ```bash # Check if API route is reachable curl -sX POST https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/api/auth \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"action":"login","email":"[REDACTED_EMAIL]","password":"test"}' | head -c 200 # Check if session cookie is set curl -sv https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/mi-cuenta 2>&1 | grep -i 'set-cookie' # Check server logs docker service logs elviajero-comercio_web --tail 30 --no-task-ids ``` ## Visual Asset Workflow ### Full Asset Overhaul Pattern When doing a complete visual assets redesign (inventory, generation, deployment): #### Phase 1: Inventory ```bash # List all files on disk find public/images -type f | sort # List all code references grep -rohP '"/images/[^"'"'"']+' --include='*.{tsx,ts,json,css}' | sed 's/^"//' | sort -u # Cross-reference: 3 outcomes per image # 1. Referenced + on disk = OK # 2. Referenced + missing = FIX (add file) # 3. On disk + no reference = ORPHAN (delete) # Size audit — small = programmatic (low quality) find public/images -name "*.png" -size -50k ``` #### Phase 2: Categorization | Type | Size threshold | Keep as | Upgrade? | |------|---------------|---------|----------| | SVGs | varies | Vector | Only if they're emoji text | | Real photos | >50KB | PNG | No | | Programmatic renders | <50KB | PNG | Yes — regenerate with richer Pillow composites | | Icons | 4-8KB | PNG | Intentionally small — minimalist silhouettes | | Hero backgrounds | <20KB | PNG | Yes — add atmosphere, silhouettes, gradients | #### Phase 3: Generation (no AI API available) Use Pillow for progressive enhancement rounds: **Round 1 — Placeholders:** ```python # Sky gradient + bokeh circles + category silhouettes # Write to a generate script, deploy, iterate ``` **Round 2 — Richer details:** - Tree silhouettes, stars, glowing tent, moon - River reflections, fishing rod, sun at horizon - Mountain trails, hiker with backpack - Headlight beams, road lines, car approaching - Dust clouds, motorcycle, fence - Fields, barn, sun, clouds **Round 3 — AI (if REPLICATE_API_TOKEN or equivalent available):** ```python if call_replicate(prompt, w, h, path): print("AI generated") else: render_fallback(path) # keep best Pillow version ``` **Round 4 — Self-critique:** After deploying, review each asset and write a brutal critique doc. Then regenerate the worst offenders in the next round. #### Phase 4: Deployment Pitfalls **Cloudflare cache (IMPORTANT):** - CF sets `cache-control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable` — 1 YEAR cache - No CF API key? Only option is **rename the file** (logo.png → logo-v2.png) - Then update ALL code references to point to new filename - Verify with curl, not browser: `curl -sk URL/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B"` - Then tell the user to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) **Docker layer cache:** - If files exist locally but return 404 on deploy, the Docker COPY was cached - Fix: `docker build --no-cache` or touch a file in the COPY layer **Verify the right file is served:** ```bash # Check HTML references curl -sk https://example.com/ | grep -oP '(logo|favicon)-v2' # Check file directly curl -sk https://example.com/images/logo-v2.png -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{size_download}B" ``` ### Key Project-Specific Patterns (El Viajero) - Product images: `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/products/*.png` (34 products) - Category backgrounds (dynamic SVG): `/images/categories/${slug}.svg` — slug values in `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` - Category heroes: heroImage prop → `app/categoria/*/page.tsx` → `/images/marketing/hero-*.png` - Hero carousel: content-driven array in `content/es.json` → `/images/marketing/hero-carousel-*.png` - Testimonials: `"image"` field in `content/es.json` → `/images/testimonials/avatar-*.png` - Logo: `header.tsx` → `/images/logo.png` (or `logo-v2.png` after cache-bust) - Favicon: `layout.tsx` → `/images/favicon.png` (or `favicon-v2.png`) - OG image: `layout.tsx` → `/images/og-viajero.svg` - PWA icons: `manifest.json` → `/images/icon-192.png`, `/images/icon-512.png` ### Generation Recipes **Category icons (128x128):** Create a Python script with `ImageDraw` — tent, fish, umbrella, backpack, car, motorcycle, barn silhouettes on green circle background. These are intentionally small (4-8KB). **Hero banners (1920x600):** Create scene composites: 1. Sky gradient (top to bottom, warm/cool depending on scene) 2. Ground layer (dark rectangle) 3. Scene elements (tree silhouettes, stars, moon, tent, river, fishing rod, mountains, road, headlights, barn, fence, sun) 4. Bokeh overlay (random circles with low alpha) 5. Gaussian blur (radius 0.3-0.5) for polish **Testimonial avatars (200x200):** If no AI available, create colored circles with basic face features (eyes, smile, hair shape, shirt). Tell the client these are placeholders for real customer photos. **PWA icons:** Render from the logo at 192x192 and 512x512 with anti-aliasing. ## Multi-Locale Content Updates (PAB Sites) When updating content across all locales (en/es/nl/de) in a PAB (Paragu-AI Builder) project: ### Safe approach vs. pitfalls 1. **Python JSON manipulation is safer than `patch` tool for large content changes.** The `patch` tool works well for small string replacements, but when replacing JSON blocks spanning 50+ lines, it can match the wrong text, leave orphaned content, and produce valid-but-wrong JSON. This happened when replacing the `processPage` section — patch matched a subset, leaving trailing orphan that was syntactically valid but structurally wrong. 2. **When `patch` goes wrong: stop, don't stack.** If the first patch produces unexpected results (orphan content, duplicated sections, lost keys), do NOT apply more patches on top. Switch immediately to Python `json.load()/json.dump()` — read the broken file, fix the data structure in memory, and write it back clean. Stacking patches compounds the mistakes and makes the file increasingly fragile to further edits. The `processPage` fix required three patches to clean up what one wrong match did. 3. **`patch` can match content from a previously-applied patch** — If a file was already modified, the old_string might not match what's actually in the file. Always read the file fresh before patching, and verify with a direct key inspection after (`python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('f')); print(d.get('targetKey',{}).keys())"`). 4. **Shell variable expansion fails in `execute_code`'s multi-line Python strings** — `for f in $FILES; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))"; done` breaks because `$f` isn't expanded inside the Python string. Use explicit file paths, a temp script, or Python's own `glob` module instead. 5. **Always validate JSON structure after content changes, not just syntax.** A file can be valid JSON but structurally wrong (missing keys, wrong step counts, orphan content). After validation: ```bash python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('file'))" # syntax check ``` Also verify structural integrity: ```python d["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] # should have 5 steps d["home"]["services"]["groups"] # should have 3 groups d["aboutPage"]["story"]["paragraphs"] # should have 3 paragraphs ``` 6. **When deleting orphaned content from a botched patch, read the file with JSON parser first**, then find the structural key that contains the orphan data. Delete it from the Python dict and re-write. Don't try to surgically remove lines with patch. 7. **Process step consistency rule:** The same process narrative lives in 6 places across the site. Update ALL of these together in the same script block — missing any one creates inconsistency: - `home.process.steps` (homepage) - `processPage.process.steps` (process page) - `landingInversor.process.steps`, `landingTrust.process.steps`, `landingLifestyle.process.steps`, `landingEmpresa.process.steps` (LinkedIn ad landing pages) 8. **Process for adding new content sections across locales:** - Step 1: Define the content data structure in a Python dict with all localizations - Step 2: Load each locale file, apply transformations, write back - Step 3: Validate JSON syntax for all files - Step 4: Verify structural keys match expectations (count groups, steps, paragraphs) ```python for lang in ["en", "es", "nl", "de"]: with open(f"content/{lang}.json") as f: d = json.load(f) d["parentSection"]["newKey"] = translations[lang] with open(f"content/{lang}.json", "w") as f: json.dump(d, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` 9. **Section structure for the 'Our Services' pattern:** ```python data["home"]["services"] = { "eyebrow": "Our Services", "title": "Section title", "groups": [ {"id": "group-id", "title": "Group Name", "subtitle": "Group subtitle.", "items": [{"title": "Service", "description": "Desc.", "icon": "IconName"}]} ] } ``` 10. **Section structure for the 'Story' pattern:** ```python data["aboutPage"]["story"] = { "title": "Our Story", "eyebrow": "Tagline", "paragraphs": ["Para 1.", "Para 2.", "Para 3."] } ``` 11. **Section structure for the 'Process steps' pattern:** ```python data["processPage"]["process"]["steps"] = [ {"number": 1, "title": "Step Name", "description": "Long description.", "icon": "IconName", "image": {"$img": "process.key"}, "duration": "Week 1"} ] ``` 12. **Landing page Calendly URL pattern:** ```python base = "https://calendly.com/nexaparaguay/consulta" utm = f"utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign={campaign}" url = f"{base}?{utm}&utm_content=cta-{placement}-{lang}" ``` ## Deployment ### Docker Deployment After modifying content assets (images, content JSON, or static files): 1. **Rebuild** — `npm run build` catches missing images and type errors 2. **Rebuild Docker image** — `docker build -t elviajero-comercio:prod .` 3. **Deploy** — `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio --detach=false` 4. **Verify** — `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com/ | head -5` 5. **Force rolling update** — if same image tag, `docker service update --force elviajero-comercio_web` Never assume changes to static content or images are live — Docker rebuild and stack deploy are required. Browser cache is NOT the server — always curl first. ### Production Monitoring After every deploy, add: - Healthcheck endpoint at `/api/health` returning `{status, timestamp, uptime}` - Docker HEALTHCHECK in Dockerfile (every 30s, 40s start grace, 3 retries) - Cron healthcheck via Hermes: every 15m, auto-redeploy on failure - Shell check script: `/root/scripts/check-{project}.sh` ### Docker Build Timeout `docker build` with npm install can take >120s on cold cache. Set terminal timeout to 300s for builds. If it times out, retry — the npm cache is warm on the second run and it will complete faster. #### Next.js `pages/` Folder Conflict (v16+) Next.js 16+ errors with "`pages` and `app` directories should be under the same folder" when a `pages/` data directory exists at project root alongside `src/app/`. Fix: 1. Rename `pages/` to `nexa-pages/` 2. Update all `page.tsx` references from `'pages'` to `'nexa-pages'` 3. Update Dockerfile to copy `nexa-pages/` into the standalone output 4. Run `docker build --no-cache` after rename (Docker layer cache may use old path) #### Docker Standalone + Data Files Next.js `output: "standalone"` produces a `server.js` that does `process.chdir(__dirname)` — so `process.cwd()` points to `.next/standalone/`, not the app root. Data files read at runtime via `readFileSync(path.join(process.cwd(), ...))` must be copied into `.next/standalone/`: ```dockerfile COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./ COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./.next/standalone/nexa-pages COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./.next/standalone/content ``` Without this, the container runs fine but returns static HTML with fallback data because `readFileSync` silently fails and the catch block returns defaults. #### The "Welcome" Bug (Static SSG Fallback) When a PAB page always renders "Welcome" regardless of content changes, the root cause is in the page component's fallback chain: ```tsx // BROKEN: home.json has 'titleKey' not 'title' — always falls through {homePage.title || content?.title || 'Welcome'} // FIXED: use siteName from the locale JSON {content?.siteName || 'Nexa Paraguay'} ``` Check that `getPages()` and `getContent()` actually read files via `readFileSync`/`readdirSync` at build time. The `catch` block silently swallowing the error is the symptom — verify the files are accessible during the Docker build stage. #### `window.gtag` Type Conflicts When a project uses `declare global { interface Window { gtag?: any } }` and imports from `@ai-whisperers/seo/analytics` (which also declares it), you get: ``` Type error: All declarations of 'gtag' must have identical modifiers. ``` **Fix:** Use `(window as any).gtag` everywhere instead of declaring `interface Window`: ```typescript const w = window as any w.gtag("event", "page_view", data) ``` Apply to all GA4 tracking files: `components/analytics.tsx`, `lib/analytics.ts`. ## Traefik + Docker Swarm Routing Setup When deploying a new Next.js client site to an existing Docker Swarm + Traefik infrastructure (paragu-ai.com VPS at 72.61.44.159): ### 1. DNS Record (Cloudflare) Add an A record in the Cloudflare dashboard for the paragu-ai.com zone: ``` Type: A Name: {site-name} Content: 72.61.44.159 Proxy status: Proxied (orange cloud) ``` ### 2. docker-compose.yml (Traefik Labels) The template's `docker-compose.yml` has superspuma's labels. Update them: ```yaml services: web: build: . image: {site-name}:prod ports: ["3000"] networks: [agent-net] deploy: mode: replicated replicas: 2 resources: limits: { memory: 512M, cpus: '0.5' } reservations: { memory: 128M, cpus: '0.1' } labels: - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.entrypoints=websecure" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.rule=Host(`{site-name}.paragu-ai.com`)" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.tls=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{site-name}.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver" - "traefik.http.services.{site-name}.loadbalancer.server.port=3000" - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net" networks: agent-net: external: true ``` Pitfall: the `image` field in docker-compose.yml must match the `docker build -t` tag. ### 3. Deploy (Manual on VPS) ```bash ssh root@72.61.44.159 cd /root/{repo-name} git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) # for @ai-whisperers npm packages npm ci --legacy-peer-deps npm run build docker build -t {site-name}:prod . docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {site-name} ``` After deploy, verify: ```bash curl -sI https://{site-name}.paragu-ai.com/ | grep "HTTP/" ``` ### 4. Docker Build GITHUB_TOKEN Issue The standard Docker multi-stage build (template's Dockerfile) runs `npm install` inside the build container but doesn't have access to the GITHUB_TOKEN for @ai-whisperers GitHub Packages registry. **Two solutions:** **Option A (simple):** Build on the host, then use a production-only Dockerfile that copies the already-built output: ```dockerfile # Dockerfile.prod — used after `npm run build` on host FROM node:20-alpine AS runner WORKDIR /app ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0" RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs . . USER nextjs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node_modules/.bin/next", "start"] ``` ```bash # On VPS: npm run build # host build has GITHUB_TOKEN docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t site:prod . # just ships node_modules + .next docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml site ``` **Option B (Docker build-arg):** Pass the token as a build arg and set it in the .npmrc at build time: ```dockerfile FROM node:20-alpine AS deps WORKDIR /app COPY package.json package-lock.json* .npmrc ./ ARG NODE_AUTH_TOKEN RUN echo "//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NODE_AUTH_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc && \ npm install --legacy-peer-deps ``` ```bash docker build --build-arg NODE_AUTH_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) -t site:prod . ``` **Pitfall:** Docker BuildKit blocks ARG values from ENV for security ("Do not use ARG or ENV instructions for sensitive data"). Option A is more reliable. ### 5. CI/CD Workflow The reusable ci-cd workflows (Ai-Whisperers/ci-cd) may not be accessible from new repos due to permissions. Example standalone deploy.yml as fallback: ```yaml name: Deploy on: push: branches: [main] jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: node-version: '20' cache: 'npm' - run: npm ci --legacy-peer-deps - run: npm run build - name: Setup SSH run: | mkdir -p ~/.ssh echo "${{ secrets.VPS_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts 2>/dev/null || true - name: Deploy via SSH run: | ssh ${{ secrets.VPS_USER }}@${{ secrets.VPS_HOST }} \ "cd /root/{repo-name} && git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/main && \ export GITHUB_TOKEN=$(gh auth token) && npm ci --legacy-peer-deps && npm run build && \ docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t {site-name}:prod . && \ docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {site-name}" ``` ## Google Maps Browser Navigation for Search-Engine-Blocked Research When search engines (Google, DuckDuckGo, Bing, Brave) block automated queries with CAPTCHAs/IP blocks, use browser_navigate to Google Maps as a workaround: ### Pattern 1. Navigate to Google Maps search URL directly: ```bash https://www.google.com/maps/search/{query}+{location} ``` 2. The browser snapshot shows results as readable `article` elements with: - Company name (clickable) - Rating (stars) - Address - Phone number - Hours - Website link (when available) 3. Click a result to view more details in the side panel. 4. To refine, clear the combobox and type a new query, then press Enter. ### Queries That Work Spanish-language queries work better for Paraguayan businesses: ``` "constructora civil Asuncion Paraguay" "vidrio templado laminado fachada Asuncion" "fachada vidrio aluminio Asuncion Paraguay" "desarrolladora inmobiliaria Asuncion Paraguay" "arquitecto Asuncion Paraguay fachada" ``` ### Limitations - Only shows ~7 results per view (Google Maps limited view without login) - No API access to extract full data programmatically - Phone numbers and emails must be copied manually from snapshots - Results are biased toward Google's ranking, not comprehensive - Works for Paraguay because Google Maps has good coverage there ## B2B Lead-Gen Conversion (e-commerce template → B2B site) When converting the e-commerce template (template-nextjs-client) into a B2B lead-generation site (construction materials, industrial supply, wholesale), follow this pattern: ### What to REMOVE from the template ``` B2C pages to delete: app/tienda/ → product store with cart/checkout app/envio/ → shipping info app/promociones/ → promotions app/producto/[slug]/ → individual product pages (replace with /productos/ catalog) B2C components to delete: search-autocomplete.tsx → no product search for B2B dark-mode-toggle.tsx → B2B stays light theme payment-methods.tsx → no payments on lead-gen site quick-order.tsx → no quick ordering product-card.tsx → replace with spec-card pattern hero-carousel.tsx → use static hero for B2B promo-carousel.tsx → no promos bottom-nav.tsx → not needed for B2B mobile empty-state.tsx → no shopping experience recently-viewed.tsx → no browsing history wishlist.ts / lib/ → no wishlists ``` ### Post-Deployment Content Audit / Roast Pattern After deploying a converted B2B site (or any new client site from template), run a systematic content audit by browsing every page live and checking for: 1. **Template remnants**: Any text/images from the original template site that leaked through (Fun4Me brand, superspuma references, wrong Instagram handles). Check /contacto, /nosotros, /faq, footer especially — these are the most commonly overlooked. 2. **Content that doesn't match the business type**: - B2B construction site still showing e-commerce order tracking - Professional services site saying "bienestar íntimo" (sex shop) - Law firm showing shipping/returns information 3. **Placeholder phone numbers**: Any hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"` (Fun4Me's number) that wasn't replaced. 4. **Broken links**: Links pointing to removed pages (/tienda, /envio, /producto/[slug]). 5. **Empty portfolio/projects**: "Coming soon" or "próximamente" on a B2B site that needs trust signals. Replace with a CTA-driven alternative. ### Content Audit / Roast Methodology Browser every page on the live site using browser_navigate and check: | Page | Common Issues | |------|---------------| | /contacto | Fun4Me Instagram, returns/orders/shipping links, wrong phone | | /nosotros | "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, sex shop story, purple heart, /tienda links | | /faq | Generic questions instead of industry-specific technical answers | | /proyectos | "Coming soon" instead of real portfolio | | /productos | Emoji icons instead of real product images | | /blog | No images, missing headers, broken formatting | After audit, fix the 3 worst pages (usually contacto, nosotros, faq) in one commit, then re-deploy and verify. ### Post-Deployment Content Audit / Roast Pattern After deploying a converted B2B site (or any new client site from template), run a systematic content audit by browsing every page live and checking for: 1. **Template remnants**: Any text/images from the original template site that leaked through (Fun4Me brand, superspuma references, wrong Instagram handles). Check /contacto, /nosotros, /faq, footer especially — these are the most commonly overlooked. 2. **Content that doesn't match the business type**: - B2B construction site still showing e-commerce order tracking - Professional services site saying "bienestar íntimo" (sex shop) - Law firm showing shipping/returns information 3. **Placeholder phone numbers**: Any hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"` (Fun4Me's number) that wasn't replaced. 4. **Broken links**: Links pointing to removed pages (/tienda, /envio, /producto/[slug]). 5. **Empty portfolio/projects**: "Coming soon" or "próximamente" on a B2B site that needs trust signals. Replace with a CTA-driven alternative. ### Content Audit / Roast Methodology Browser every page on the live site using browser_navigate and check: | Page | Common Issues | |------|---------------| | /contacto | Fun4Me Instagram, returns/orders/shipping links, wrong phone | | /nosotros | "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, sex shop story, purple heart, /tienda links | | /faq | Generic questions instead of industry-specific technical answers | | /proyectos | "Coming soon" instead of real portfolio | | /productos | Emoji icons instead of real product images | | /blog | No images, missing headers, broken formatting | After audit, fix the 3 worst pages (usually contacto, nosotros, faq) in one commit, then re-deploy and verify. ### Full list of Fun4Me artifacts to hunt when converting template When converting the template to a B2B site, these Fun4Me-specific traces survive even after page/component deletion: | File | What to fix | |------|-------------| | `app/contacto/page.tsx` | Hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"`, Instagram `@fun4me_py`, "Estado del pedido" / "Reclamo / devolución" quick links | | `app/nosotros/page.tsx` | `brand.story` fallback to "bienestar íntimo con total privacidad" (sex shop), "¿Por qué Fun4Me?" section, purple heart emoji, `/tienda` link | | `app/sitemap.ts` | Hardcoded `base = "https://fun4me.paragu-ai.com"`, fun4me page paths | | `content/es.json` | `analytics.ga4` empty string (needs real GA4 ID), `home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref: "/tienda"` | | `layout.tsx` | Theme-color `#1B5E20` (green, not blue B2B), metadata title fallbacks | | `components/` | Any file still importing `@/components/search-autocomplete` or `dark-mode-toggle` | | `docker-compose.yml` | Traefik labels referencing old site name (`superspuma`) | | Blog post `[slug]/page.tsx` | Hardcoded `WA_PHONE = "[REDACTED_PHONE]"` | After content/es.json fixes, check the about/nosotros pages still reference `brand` keys (which were Fun4Me-specific) instead of `nosotros` keys (which have the B2B content). Fix by rewriting page.tsx to use the correct content section. | Page | Purpose | Content Pattern | |------|---------|-----------------| | /productos | Product catalog with specs | Technical data + "request quote" CTA (no prices) | | /proyectos | Portfolio gallery | Image + building name + scope + year | | /servicios | Service listing | 5-6 service cards with icons | | /cotizar | Quote request form | Name, company, email, phone, project type, product type (multi-select), qty, timeline → WhatsApp | ### Content Model (es.json) Home page sections for B2B: 1. **Hero**: gradient background, headline focusing on B2B value prop, two CTAs (quote form + WhatsApp) 2. **Features grid**: 4-column cards (stock local, certified quality, tech support, payment terms) 3. **Product preview**: 4 cards linking to /productos anchors 4. **Testimonials**: 2-3 B2B client quotes 5. **Final CTA**: Dark gradient, WhatsApp button Product catalog structure: ```json { "products": [ { "id": "product-slug", "name": "Product Name", "description": "Technical description for B2B buyers", "features": ["Feature 1", "Feature 2"], "applications": ["Building type 1", "Building type 2"], "cta": "Request quote for X" } ] } ``` Quote form on /cotizar: collects name, company, email, phone, project type (dropdown), product type (multi-select buttons), approximate qty/m2, timeline, message. On submit → opens WhatsApp with pre-formatted message. ### Theme Use a LIGHT theme for construction/B2B (not dark): ```json { "primary": "#0F62FE", "secondary": "#1A1A2E", "accent": "#00A3E0", "background": "#FFFFFF", "surface": "#F8F9FA" } ``` Remove dark-mode-toggle. The template already supports light mode via content/tokens.json `"theme": "light"`. ### Header/Footer Adaptation Header: replace Fun4Me brand with client name. Replace nav items. Footer: replace brand name, update columns for B2B context. ### @ai-whisperers Package Version Debugging When the template has `@ai-whisperers/*` dependencies with `^0.1.0` ranges that don't match the published registry: 1. Check actual latest versions: ```bash for pkg in @ai-whisperers/ui @ai-whisperers/admin @ai-whisperers/seo; do echo -n "$pkg: "; npm view $pkg version 2>&1 done ``` 2. Published versions differ per package: | Package | Latest | |---------|--------| | @ai-whisperers/ui | 0.4.0 | | @ai-whisperers/admin | 0.2.1 | | @ai-whisperers/seo | 0.2.0 | | @ai-whisperers/theme | 0.2.0 | | @ai-whisperers/auth | 0.2.0 | | @ai-whisperers/commerce | 0.2.0 | | @ai-whisperers/whatsapp | 0.2.0 | | @ai-whisperers/i18n | 0.2.0 | 3. When auditing a Next.js e-commerce site with add-to-cart that doesn't work, the #1 root cause is **CartProvider not in the root layout and not shared across all pages**.

{"success": true, "name": "nextjs-docker-swarm", "description": "Build, deploy, and operate Next.js apps on Docker Swarm with Traefik ingress — covers image builds with private npm registries, workspace packages, Supabase key management, multi-replica deploys, and production debugging.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: nextjs-docker-swarm
description: Build, deploy, and operate Next.js apps on Docker Swarm with Traefik ingress — covers image builds with private npm registries, workspace packages, Supabase key management, multi-replica deploys, and production debugging.
---

# Next.js Docker Swarm Deployment

Class-level skill for deploying any Next.js app to Docker Swarm with Traefik. Covers patterns shared across client sites (El Viajero, Nexa Paraguay, etc.).

## Project Layout

Typical structure:
```
/root/<project>/
├── Dockerfile            # Multi-stage: deps -> builder -> runner
├── docker-compose.yml    # Swarm stack definition
├── deploy.sh             # build + docker build + stack deploy
├── .env.local            # NEXT_PUBLIC_* vars
├── .env                  # Secrets (non-public)
├── packages/             # Local workspace packages (if no private registry)
├── content/es.json       # Static content (may contain build-time config)
└── CLAUDE.md             # Agent guide
```

## Dockerfile Pattern

Standard multi-stage for Next.js standalone output. Three variants — pick based on your dependency strategy.

### Variant A: Private npm registry (GitHub Packages)

Use when `@scope/*` packages are on a private registry.

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
ARG NODE_AUTH_TOKEN
ENV NODE_AUTH_TOKEN=$NODE_AUTH_TOKEN
RUN echo "@scope:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com" > .npmrc && \
    echo "//npm.pkg.github.com/:_authToken=${NODE_AUTH_TOKEN}" >> .npmrc && \
    npm install --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
RUN apk add --no-cache wget curl
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
HEALTHCHECK CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/api/health || exit 1
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
CMD node server.js
```

### Variant B: Local workspace packages (no private registry)

Use when all dependencies come from npm or local workspace packages. The deps stage copies workspace packages so npm install resolves them locally.

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
COPY packages/ ./packages/
RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=deps /app/packages ./packages
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:22-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN apk add --no-cache wget curl
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
RUN rm -rf node_modules/.cache node_modules/typescript
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
HEALTHCHECK CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/api/health || exit 1
CMD node server.js
```

**Key differences from Variant A:** No ARG NODE_AUTH_TOKEN, no .npmrc creation, workspace packages copied into deps so npm resolves them, size prune runs as root before USER nextjs (explicit rm of TypeScript and .cache), no NEXT_PUBLIC_* ARG in runner stage (use runtime env instead).

### Variant C: Debian-slim (SWC compatibility, host pre-install)

**Use when:** Alpine builds fail with `Turbopack is not supported on this platform (linux/x64) because native bindings are not available. Only WebAssembly (WASM) bindings were loaded` — the `@next/swc-linux-x64-gnu` binary needs full glibc and `libc6-compat` on Alpine may not be sufficient.

Also use when the GitHub Packages token is a fine-grained PAT (`github_pat_*`) that lacks `read:packages` scope. Detect via:
```bash
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $GITHUB_TOKEN" https://api.github.com/user | jq .oauth_scopes
# Classic PAT (ghp_*): ["repo","read:packages"] — works in Docker
# Fine-grained PAT (github_pat_*): null — fails with 401
```

IMPORTANT: Fine-grained PATs (`github_pat_*`) CANNOT authenticate to `npm.pkg.github.com` even with full repo/admin permissions granted via the UI. The only fix is a **classic PAT** (`ghp_*`) from `github.com/settings/tokens` with `repo` + `read:packages` + `workflow` scopes. Classic PATs CANNOT be created via the API or from the fine-grained PAT beta page — must be done manually at the classic token page.

This should be updated in the Variant C Dockerfile below when detected.

```dockerfile
FROM node:22-slim AS deps
RUN corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@latest --activate
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json pnpm-lock.yaml pnpm-workspace.yaml ./
COPY node_modules ./node_modules

FROM node:22-slim AS build
RUN corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@latest --activate
WORKDIR /app
RUN apt-get update -qq && apt-get install -y -qq wget && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN npx next build

FROM node:22-slim AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs && \
    apt-get update -qq && apt-get install -y -qq wget && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY --from=build --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=build --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=build --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=build --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
HEALTHCHECK CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 -O /dev/null http://127.0.0.1:3000/admin/content || exit 1
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

Key differences from Alpine variant:
- `node:22-slim` (Debian) — SWC native bindings work natively, no `libc6-compat` needed
- Deps are pre-installed on host and copied in (workaround for private npm registry auth)
- Uses pnpm (most Next.js 16 projects use pnpm)
- Healthcheck pings a known-good route like `/admin/content` — root `/` may 404 with the Next 16 Turbopack route group bug

## Build & Deploy (Single Service — service update pattern)

For projects deployed as a single service (not a full stack), use the `docker build && docker service update` pattern:

```bash
# Build
docker build -t <project>:prod .

# Deploy (forces rolling update)
docker service update --image <project>:prod <stack>_web
# or with --force to guarantee new tasks even with same tag
docker service update --force <stack>_web

# Verify
sleep 5
curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://example.com
```

**When to use this vs `docker stack deploy`:**
- Use `service update` when the stack is already deployed and you only changed the image
- Use `stack deploy` for first-time deploy, infrastructure changes, or multi-service stack updates
- `service update --force` restarts tasks even when the image tag hasn't changed (same-tag detection issue, see pitfall #16)

**Verification checklist:**
```bash
# Health check
curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://example.com

# Check key routes
for route in es/ es/servicios es/sobre es/feedback; do
  code=$(curl -sL -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://example.com/$route")
  echo "$code - /$route"
done
```

## Build & Deploy (Full Stack — stack deploy pattern)

```bash
# Build with all required build-args
docker build \
  -t <project>:prod \
  --build-arg NODE_AUTH_TOKEN="ghp_xxx" \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL="https://xxx.supabase.co" \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="eyJ..." \
  .

# Deploy stack
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <stack-name> --with-registry-auth --resolve-image always
```

## Compose Template (Traefik + Swarm)

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    image: <project>:prod
    ports:
      - "3000"
    networks:
      - agent-net
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
      - NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://xxx.supabase.co
      - NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=eyJ...
      - SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=eyJ...
      - NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL=https://example.com
    deploy:
      mode: replicated
      replicas: 2
      labels:
        - "traefik.enable=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.<name>.entrypoints=websecure"
        - "traefik.http.routers.<name>.rule=Host(`example.com`)"
        - "traefik.http.routers.<name>.tls=true"
        - "traefik.http.routers.<name>.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver"
        - "traefik.http.services.<name>.loadbalancer.server.port=3000"
        - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net"
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '0.5'
          memory: 512M
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"]
networks:
  agent-net:
    external: true
```

## Orphan Container Detection

Before deploying, check for orphan `docker-compose` containers that can interfere with Swarm:

```bash
# List ALL containers matching the project name (not just swarm tasks)
docker ps -a --filter "name=<project>" --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}"

# Look for containers named like <project>-web-2 (compose-style) vs <project>_web.1 (swarm-style)
# Compose-style containers are NOT managed by Swarm and will NOT be updated by stack deploy
```

If you find orphans:

```bash
# 1. Identify ALL containers
docker ps -a --filter "name=<project>" --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.CreatedAt}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}"

# 2. Check if the orphan is on the same Docker network
docker network inspect agent-net --format '{{range .Containers}}{{.Name}} -> {{.IPv4Address}}{{println}}{{end}}' | grep <project>

# 3. Kill the orphan
docker rm -f <orphan-container-name>

# 4. Remove old images so nothing can revive them
docker rmi <old-image-id>

# 5. Verify only swarm replicas remain
docker service ps <stack> --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.CurrentState}}\t{{.Image}}"
docker ps --filter "name=<project>" --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.CreatedAt}}\t{{.Status}}"
```

## Critical Pitfalls

### 1. Private npm Registry Auth (NODE_AUTH_TOKEN vs .npmrc)

The Dockerfile needs npm auth for `@scope/*` packages from GitHub Packages. Three approaches:

**A) Docker build arg (works with classic PATs):**
Pass `--build-arg NODE_AUTH_TOKEN=ghp_xxx`. The Dockerfile writes `.npmrc` from this arg.

**B) Host pre-install + COPY (when fine-grained PAT lacks read:packages):**
If the GITHUB_TOKEN is a fine-grained PAT that doesn't have `read:packages` scope, install fails with 401. Workaround: install deps on the host, then COPY node_modules into the deps stage. The `.dockerignore` must NOT exclude `node_modules`.

**Detection:** Docker build fails with `ERR_PNPM_FETCH_401` on `npm.pkg.github.com`.

**PAT scope detection:** `curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://api.github.com/user | jq .oauth_scopes` returns `null` for fine-grained PATs (token is valid but scope-less for npm), vs `["repo","read:packages"]` for classic PATs. Fine-grained PATs CANNOT download npm packages from GitHub Packages.

**C) Eliminate the registry entirely (best when possible):**
If you control the packages, extract them into local workspace packages. Removes the NODE_AUTH_TOKEN dependency. See `references/el-viajero-revamp-[REDACTED_PHONE].md`.

### 2. Env Variable Name Mismatch

Docker Compose `environment:` keys must match `.env` file keys exactly. Run `docker stack deploy` from the same directory as `.env` so Docker picks up the variables automatically.

### 3. Minimal Private Registry Alternative: Local Workspace Packages

Instead of managing a private npm registry + NODE_AUTH_TOKEN, you can eliminate both by creating local workspace packages. This works when:

- The packages are internal (only consumed by your projects)
- You have the package source or can copy the compiled dist/ from node_modules
- The imports in your source code use the same package name/path

**How to migrate:**
1. Create `packages/<name>/package.json` with exports map matching the original package's subpaths
2. Copy `dist/` from old `node_modules` to the workspace package
3. Add `"workspaces": ["packages/*"]` to root package.json
4. Keep the original package name in dependencies (npm workspace symlinks resolve them)
5. Update Dockerfile to copy `packages/` before npm install (Variant B)

**Risk:** The workspace package's `"use client"` components may crash SSR. See pitfall #13 below.

### 4. Content JSON as Config Store

Some apps read Supabase URL/anon key from `content/*.json` via static JSON imports. This means the key is **baked into the Next.js build** at build time. Changing `.env.local` alone won't fix it — you must also rebuild.

### 5. Build Fails But Old Containers Keep Running

If `docker build` fails, the old image stays tagged as `:prod`. Existing containers continue on the old image. This is safer than docker-compose which would restart failed containers.

### 6. Traefik Compression Middleware

Add gzip/brotli compression via two deploy labels:

```yaml
deploy:
  labels:
    - "traefik.http.middlewares.{name}-compress.compress=true"
    - "traefik.http.routers.{name}.middlewares={name}-compress"
```

Compress middleware handles gzip + brotli automatically. Saves 70-80% on large JSON payloads.

### 7. Traefik Labels at Wrong Level

Traefik labels must be at `service.deploy.labels`, NOT at `service.labels`. Docker Swarm ignores `service.labels`.

### 8. NEXT_PUBLIC_* Not Passed to Runner Stage

If the builder stage uses NEXT_PUBLIC_* as build args but the runner stage doesn't have ENV entries for them, the server can't connect at runtime. Verification: `docker inspect <container> | grep NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE`

### 9. Pages Router vs App Router (Next.js 16)

Key rules for Next.js 16 with Turbopack:
- Both `pages/` and `app/` can coexist but Next.js 16 prefers `pages/`
- `[[...slug]].tsx` may silently produce zero output
- Route group root page bug: `(store)/page.tsx` may compile but not register. Fix: move to `app/page.tsx`
- Middleware file is deprecated — use `proxy` instead
- Use `next/navigation` (`usePathname`, `useSearchParams`) instead of `next/router`

### 10. Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR) — Pitfalls

When PPR is enabled with `cacheComponents: true` in `next.config.js`:
- Every uncached data access MUST be inside Suspense
- All route segment configs are forbidden (PPR handles them)
- `generateStaticParams` is required for every dynamic route
- `generateMetadata` must NOT access uncached data
- Build output shows `◐` (Partial Prerender) vs `●` (SSG) and `ƒ` (Dynamic)

**PPR static shell trap:** The PPR build produces a static HTML shell with an empty `<main>` tag. The dynamic content (hero, sections, data) is serialized into `<script>` tags as an RSC (React Server Component) payload and streamed via JavaScript. This means:
- `curl` or `wget` to check if content renders will find `<main></main>` — misleading
- Content IS present in the HTML, but embedded in `self.__next_f.push([...])` script arrays
- The browser snapshot tool or DOM inspection before JS hydrates shows "empty page"
- **Verification:** search for actual content strings (headlines, section titles) in the full HTML output, not just inside `<main>`
- **Alternative:** Disable PPR entirely by removing `cacheComponents: true` from `next.config.js`. Routes become `● Static` with content fully rendered in HTML

**When to disable PPR vs keep it:**
- **Keep PPR** when you need instant first-contentful-paint for SEO-critical pages and can accept the JS-dependency for full render
- **Disable PPR** when your content is static (JSON-driven, not user-specific), because the static HTML output is identical but simpler to debug

**How to disable PPR:**
1. Remove `cacheComponents: true` from `next.config.js`
2. Remove `<Suspense>` wrappers from page components — just render the content directly
3. Rebuild (`rm -rf .next && npm run build`)
4. Confirm build output shows `●` (Static) instead of `◐` (Partial Prerender)

### 11. Hermes Cron Jobs With Stale Workdirs

Cron jobs store a `workdir` that may reference the original repo path. If the repo was renamed or moved, the cron tries to `cd` into the old path. A healthcheck cron may detect the site is "down" and attempt a redeploy from the wrong path.

**Check:** `hermes cron list | grep -A 5 <project>` and look for `workdir`
**Fix:** `cronjob(action="edit", job_id="...", workdir="/root/current-path")`

### 12. Healthcheck Route Selection

Must ping a route returning HTTP 200-399. Test locally first: `docker run --rm -d -p 13000:3000 <image>` then `curl -I http://localhost:13000/<route>`.

### 13. GITHUB_TOKEN PAT Type Detection

When Docker build fails with `ERR_PNPM_FETCH_401` on `npm.pkg.github.com`, check the PAT type:

```bash
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $GITHUB_TOKEN" https://api.github.com/user | jq .oauth_scopes
```

- **Classic PAT (`ghp_*`)** — returns `["repo", "read:packages"]`. The `.npmrc` auth works in Docker.
- **Fine-grained PAT (`github_pat_*`)** — returns `null`. Token works for `git clone/push` and `gh api` but CANNOT authenticate to `npm.pkg.github.com` for package downloads, even with all repo permissions granted via the fine-grained UI.

**Workaround (fine-grained PAT):** Install deps on host, disable `.dockerignore`'s node_modules exclusion, COPY node_modules into build context, skip npm/pnpm install in Dockerfile. See `references/fun4me-recovery-[REDACTED_PHONE].md`.

**Permanent fix:** Create a **classic PAT** at `https://github.com/settings/tokens` with `repo` + `read:packages` + `workflow` scopes. This MUST be done manually in the browser — the API and the fine-grained PAT beta page at `github.com/settings/tokens?type=beta` can only create fine-grained PATs which won't work for npm package downloads.

### 14. El Viajero-Style OOM (Exit 137 on Replica)

**Symptom:** One replica runs fine, the second exits with `non-zero exit (137)` = SIGKILL (OOM-killed).

**Root cause:** Node.js memory limit + Docker resource limit. Next.js SSR under load can exceed 512MB.

**Immediate fix:** Scale to 1 replica to stabilize:
```bash
docker service scale <service>=1
```

**Permanent fix:** Increase the container memory limit in docker-compose.yml:
```yaml
deploy:
  resources:
    limits:
      memory: 1024M  # was 512M
```

Or set Node.js heap limit:
```yaml
environment:
  - NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=768
```

### 15. .dockerignore Excluding Source Directories

**Symptom:** Docker build fails with `Couldn't find any 'pages' or 'app' directory. Please create one under the project root`, even though `src/app/` exists with valid route files.

**Root cause:** The `.dockerignore` file excludes `src/app` (or other source directories). `COPY . .` in the builder stage ignores everything listed in `.dockerignore`, so the build context arrives without the app directory.

**Why it happens:** `.dockerignore` entries accumulate across sessions. A common sequence: someone adds `src/app` to `.dockerignore` during a past refactor (e.g., when app was at root level), the project later moves to `src/app/` or `app/`, the `.dockerignore` is never updated.

**Check:**
```bash
cat .dockerignore
# Look for lines listing src/app, app/ or other source directories
```

**Fix:** Remove any source directory entries from `.dockerignore`. Typical clean `.dockerignore`:
```
node_modules
.next
.git
```

**Verification after fix:**
```bash
docker build --no-cache -t <project>:test . 2>&1 | tail -5
# Should complete successfully
```

**Prevention:** When restructuring a Next.js project (moving from root-level to `src/` layout), audit the `.dockerignore` immediately after the move. The silent build failure only shows up on next deploy, which may be days or weeks later.

**Build fails silently? Check exit code:**
```bash
docker build -t <project>:prod . 2>&1 | tail -20
# Look for ERROR lines, not just the last output line
# A build may print 'DONE' for earlier stages but fail on the build stage
```

### 16. Same-Tag Image Not Detected by Swarm

When you rebuild an image with the same tag (e.g. `elviajero:prod`), `docker stack deploy` does NOT detect the change. The old containers keep running because Swarm sees the tag hasn't changed.

**Symptom:** You rebuilt, you redeployed, but the live site still shows old content/logos.

**Verification:**
```bash
docker images elviajero:prod --format "table {{.Repository}}\t{{.Tag}}\t{{.ID}}\t{{.CreatedAt}}"
```
If the image ID changed but running containers show the old one, Swarm didn't pick it up.

**Alternative: Scale down/up (100% restart guarantee):**\n```bash\ndocker service scale <service>=0\nsleep 5\ndocker service scale <service>=<replicas>\n```\nThis kills all tasks and creates fresh ones. 100% effective even when `--force` doesn't work. Brief downtime (~5-10s).\n\n**Fix — Force rolling update:**
```bash
docker service update --force elviajero_web
```
Or tag with a unique identifier:
```bash
docker tag elviajero:prod elviajero:prod-$(date +%s)
docker service update --image elviajero:prod elviajero_web
```
`--force` triggers a rolling restart even when the tag is identical. Works on single-node Swarm.

**Alternative: Scale down/up (100% restart guarantee):**
When `--force` doesn't work (rare but happens with same-tag detection), scale down to 0 and back up:
```bash
docker service scale <service>=0
sleep 3
docker service scale <service>=<replicas>
```
This kills ALL tasks and creates fresh ones. Brief downtime (~5-10s) but 100% reliable. Verify with:
```bash
docker service ps <stack> --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.CurrentState}}\t{{.Image}}"
docker ps --filter "name=<project>" --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.CreatedAt}}\t{{.Image}}"
```

### 17. Supabase Key Rotation: JWT → new-format keys

Supabase now supports two key formats. If you see `Invalid API key` with a previously-working JWT:

**Old format:** `eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIs...` (JWT, 217 chars)
**New format:** `sb_secret_*` or `sb_publishable_*` (42+ chars)

**Detection:**
```bash
# Test old JWT format
curl -s "$URL/rest/v1/$TABLE?select=count" \
  -H "apikey: $OLD_JWT_KEY"

# Test new format (works as both apikey and bearer)
curl -s "$URL/rest/v1/$TABLE?select=count" \
  -H "apikey: sb_secret_XXXXX" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sb_secret_XXXXX"
```

**Fix:** Get both keys from Supabase Dashboard > Settings > API > Project API keys.
- `sb_publishable_*` = anon/public key (safe to hardcode in docker-compose)
- `sb_secret_*` = service_role key (must be kept secret)

Update all 3 locations: `.env`, `docker-compose.yml`, and (if applicable) `content/*.json`.

### 18. Database Seeding Pattern

For creating sample data in Supabase for admin testing:

**Generate seed SQL from content file:**
```bash
python3 scripts/seed-data.py
```
The script reads `content/es.json` and generates INSERT statements for all tables (categories, products, reviews, orders, promo codes, B2B customers, subscribers).

**Apply via Supabase Dashboard:**
1. Go to https://supabase.com/dashboard/project/<project-id>
2. SQL Editor
3. Paste generated SQL from `/tmp/*-seed.sql`
4. Run

**Or seed via Supabase REST API (programmatic, no dashboard needed):**
```bash
# Write payload to a file to avoid bash escaping hell
cat > /tmp/payload.json << 'JSONEOF'
[{"id":"ORD-001","items":"...","total":"450000","status":"entregado"}]
JSONEOF

curl -s -X POST "$URL/rest/v1/$TABLE" \
  -H "apikey: $SVC_KEY" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SVC_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Prefer: resolution=merge-duplicates" \
  --data-binary @/tmp/payload.json
```

`resolution=merge-duplicates` acts like `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` for REST inserts.

**Order items JSON field pitfall:** Supabase stores `ej_orders.items` as a raw JSON **string**, not a parsed JSONB array. Calling `order.items.length` returns the character count, not the item count. Always parse: `JSON.parse(order.items).length`. Wrap in try/catch for safety since unauthenticated users get empty objects from `useAuth()`.

**useAuth() destructure crash pattern:** When `useAuth()` returns `{}` (no user logged in — happens during SSR, RSC boundary issues, or unauthenticated state), ALL destructured values are `undefined`. Every component that calls `const { user, orders, addresses, favorites } = useAuth()` MUST provide defaults:

```typescript
// BAD — crashes with "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'length')"
const { user, orders, addresses } = useAuth()
orders.length  // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined

// GOOD — provides fallbacks
const { user = null, orders = [], addresses = [], favorites = [] } = useAuth()
```

Apply this pattern to ALL pages that destructure from useAuth: mi-cuenta, checkout, login, register, pedidos, favoritos, direcciones, beneficios, cancelar, configuracion, confirmado.

### 19. Admin Pages Healthcheck Pattern

When verifying a site's admin panel, check ALL admin pages return 200:
```bash
for path in admin admin/productos admin/pedidos admin/clientes admin/blog \
  admin/contenido admin/categorias admin/suscriptores admin/b2b admin/reportes \
  admin/promos admin/resenas admin/usuarios admin/tema admin/fotos admin/importar admin/enriquecer; do
  code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://example.com/$path")
  echo "$code - $path"
done
```
Note: 200 does not mean the page shows data — just that it rendered. Empty tables with skeleton loaders also return 200.

Next.js 15/16 RSC boundary issue: Even when the page correctly wraps content in `<AuthProvider>`, React Server Component boundaries can cause `useContext(AuthContext)` to return `null` at runtime. The SSR guard doesn't catch this because `window` exists. If the console shows `useAuth must be used within AuthProvider` but the component tree has the provider, permanently remove the throw and always return empty object from `useAuth()`.
```bash
python3 /root/.hermes/skills/devops/nextjs-docker-swarm/templates/generate-seed-sql.py content/es.json > /tmp/seed.sql
```

**Important:** Anon key works for reads but NOT writes. Service role key is needed for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. If the service role key is truncated in `.env`, get it fresh from Supabase Dashboard > Settings > API.

### 18. Migrating from Private Registry to Local Workspace Packages

**Situation:** The project depends on `@scope/*` packages only available on GitHub Packages private registry. The build requires NODE_AUTH_TOKEN which breaks CI and creates a fragile dependency.

**Migration steps:**

1. **Check what the source code actually imports** from the package:
```bash
grep -rh '@scope/package/' --include='*.{ts,tsx}' . | sed 's/.*from "//;s/\".*//' | sort -u
```

2. **Create workspace package** with only the needed exports:
```json
{
  "name": "@scope/package",
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "exports": {
    "./subpath-a": { "import": "./dist/subpath-a.js", "types": "./dist/subpath-a.d.ts" }
  }
}
```

3. **Copy ONLY the needed dist files** from `node_modules/@scope/package/dist/`

4. **Add workspace to root package.json:** `"workspaces": ["packages/*"]`
   Keep `"@scope/package"` in dependencies — npm workspace symlinks resolve locally.

5. **Update Dockerfile** to Variant B pattern (copy packages/ before npm install)

6. **Delete .npmrc**

7. **Verify:** `npm install` works without private registry, `npm run build` succeeds.

## Database Troubleshooting

### Admin login shows "Invalid API key"
1. Check the anon key against Supabase dashboard
2. Update all 3 locations (.env.local, docker-compose, content/*.json)
3. Rebuild Docker image
4. Redeploy
5. Force rolling update if image tag unchanged

### Admin login shows "Invalid login credentials"
Key is correct but password is wrong. Reset via Supabase Admin API:
```bash
curl -X PUT "$SUPABASE_URL/auth/v1/admin/users/$USER_ID" \
  -H "apikey: $SVC_KEY" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SVC_KEY" \
  -d '{"password":"newpass"}'
```

### Login works but admin panel shows no data
Check `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` is set in the running container. Without it, admin API routes can't read profiles due to RLS policies.

### Admin pages show empty tables
The admin pages work (HTTP 200) but need sample data. Run the seed SQL script (pitfall #19) to populate products, orders, reviews, subscribers, B2B customers, and promo codes.

## Adding to CLAUDE.md

Every project should have a `CLAUDE.md` at the repo root with:
- Quick links (live URL, repo, local path, VPS, Traefik host rules)
- Architecture summary (CF -> VPS -> Traefik -> service)
- Pages list
- Design system notes
- Build & deploy commands
- Critical patterns (e.g., WhatsApp-first ordering)

When migrating `@scope/auth` to a local workspace, static generation may crash with:
```
Error: useAuth must be used within AuthProvider
```

**Root cause:** The workspace package's `useContext(AuthContext)` returns `null` during SSR because the provider tree doesn't exist yet on first render.

**Fix — Two layers:**

**Layer 1 — SSR guard (prevents build crash):**
```typescript
export function useAuth() {
  const ctx = useContext(AuthContext)
  if (!ctx) {
    if (typeof window === "undefined") return {} as AuthCtx
    throw new Error("useAuth must be used within AuthProvider")
  }
  return ctx
}
```

**Layer 2 — Client-side fallback (prevents runtime crash from RSC boundary issues):**
Even with correct DOM tree placement of `<AuthProvider>`, Next.js 15/16 RSC boundaries can cause the provider context to not be visible to client components. In this case, the SSR guard passes but `useContext` returns `null` at runtime. The error message appears in the browser console as a stack trace starting from the page's client chunk.

If this happens: **remove the throw entirely** and always return an empty object:
```typescript
export function useAuth() {
  const ctx = useContext(AuthContext)
  if (!ctx) return {} as AuthCtx
  return ctx
}
```
This means components wrapping `useAuth` must handle the `user === null` case gracefully (which they already do since unauthenticated users are the normal state), AND they MUST provide defaults when destructuring:
```typescript
const { user = null, orders = [], addresses = [], favorites = [] } = useAuth()
```

**Detection:** Check the browser console for `useAuth must be used within AuthProvider`. If it only appears on one page but the component tree has `<AuthProvider>` wrapping the content, it's an RSC boundary issue.

name: dogfood
description: Systematic exploratory QA testing of web applications — find bugs, capture evidence, and generate structured reports
version: 1.0.0
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [qa, testing, browser, web, dogfood]
    related_skills: []

# Dogfood: Systematic Web Application QA Testing

## Overview

This skill guides you through systematic exploratory QA testing of web applications using the browser toolset. You will navigate the application, interact with elements, capture evidence of issues, and produce a structured bug report.

## Prerequisites

- Browser toolset must be available (`browser_navigate`, `browser_snapshot`, `browser_click`, `browser_type`, `browser_vision`, `browser_console`, `browser_scroll`, `browser_back`, `browser_press`, `browser_close`)
- A target URL and testing scope from the user

## Inputs

The user provides:
1. **Target URL** — the entry point for testing
2. **Scope** — what areas/features to focus on (or "full site" for comprehensive testing)
3. **Output directory** (optional) — where to save screenshots and the report (default: `./dogfood-output`)

## Workflow

Follow this 5-phase systematic workflow:

### Phase 1: Plan

1. Create the output directory structure:
   ```
   {output_dir}/
   ├── screenshots/       # Evidence screenshots
   └── report.md          # Final report (generated in Phase 5)
   ```
2. Identify the testing scope based on user input.
3. Build a rough sitemap by planning which pages and features to test:
   - Landing/home page
   - Navigation links (header, footer, sidebar)
   - Key user flows (sign up, login, search, checkout, etc.)
   - Forms and interactive elements
   - Edge cases (empty states, error pages, 404s)

### Phase 2: Explore

For each page or feature in your plan:

1. **Navigate** to the page:
   ```
   browser_navigate(url="https://example.com/page")
   ```

2. **Take a snapshot** to understand the DOM structure:
   ```
   browser_snapshot()
   ```

3. **Check the console** for JavaScript errors:
   ```
   browser_console(clear=true)
   ```
   Do this after every navigation and after every significant interaction. Silent JS errors are high-value findings.

4. **Take an annotated screenshot** to visually assess the page and identify interactive elements:
   ```
   browser_vision(question="Describe the page layout, identify any visual issues, broken elements, or accessibility concerns", annotate=true)
   ```
   The `annotate=true` flag overlays numbered `[N]` labels on interactive elements. Each `[N]` maps to ref `@eN` for subsequent browser commands.

5. **Test interactive elements** systematically:
   - Click buttons and links: `browser_click(ref="@eN")`
   - Fill forms: `browser_type(ref="@eN", text="test input")`
   - Test keyboard navigation: `browser_press(key="Tab")`, `browser_press(key="Enter")`
   - Scroll through content: `browser_scroll(direction="down")`
   - Test form validation with invalid inputs
   - Test empty submissions

6. **After each interaction**, check for:
   - Console errors: `browser_console()`
   - Visual changes: `browser_vision(question="What changed after the interaction?")`
   - Expected vs actual behavior

### Phase 3: Collect Evidence

For every issue found:

1. **Take a screenshot** showing the issue:
   ```
   browser_vision(question="Capture and describe the issue visible on this page", annotate=false)
   ```
   Save the `screenshot_path` from the response — you will reference it in the report.

2. **Record the details**:
   - URL where the issue occurs
   - Steps to reproduce
   - Expected behavior
   - Actual behavior
   - Console errors (if any)
   - Screenshot path

3. **Classify the issue** using the issue taxonomy (see `references/issue-taxonomy.md`):
   - Severity: Critical / High / Medium / Low
   - Category: Functional / Visual / Accessibility / Console / UX / Content

### Phase 4: Categorize

1. Review all collected issues.
2. De-duplicate — merge issues that are the same bug manifesting in different places.
3. Assign final severity and category to each issue.
4. Sort by severity (Critical first, then High, Medium, Low).
5. Count issues by severity and category for the executive summary.

### Phase 5: Report

Generate the final report using the template at `templates/dogfood-report-template.md`.

The report must include:
1. **Executive summary** with total issue count, breakdown by severity, and testing scope
2. **Per-issue sections** with:
   - Issue number and title
   - Severity and category badges
   - URL where observed
   - Description of the issue
   - Steps to reproduce
   - Expected vs actual behavior
   - Screenshot references (use `MEDIA:<screenshot_path>` for inline images)
   - Console errors if relevant
3. **Summary table** of all issues
4. **Testing notes** — what was tested, what was not, any blockers

Save the report to `{output_dir}/report.md`.

## Tools Reference

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `browser_navigate` | Go to a URL |
| `browser_snapshot` | Get DOM text snapshot (accessibility tree) |
| `browser_click` | Click an element by ref (`@eN`) or text |
| `browser_type` | Type into an input field |
| `browser_scroll` | Scroll up/down on the page |
| `browser_back` | Go back in browser history |
| `browser_press` | Press a keyboard key |
| `browser_vision` | Screenshot + AI analysis; use `annotate=true` for element labels |
| `browser_console` | Get JS console output and errors |
| `browser_close` | Close the browser session |

## Tips

- **Always check `browser_console()` after navigating and after significant interactions.** Silent JS errors are among the most valuable findings.
- **Use `annotate=true` with `browser_vision`** when you need to reason about interactive element positions or when the snapshot refs are unclear.
- **Test with both valid and invalid inputs** — form validation bugs are common.
- **Scroll through long pages** — content below the fold may have rendering issues.
- **Test navigation flows** — click through multi-step processes end-to-end.
- **Check responsive behavior** by noting any layout issues visible in screenshots.
- **Don't forget edge cases**: empty states, very long text, special characters, rapid clicking.
- When reporting screenshots to the user, include `MEDIA:<screenshot_path>` so they can see the evidence inline.
- **DOM snapshot limitation**: `browser_snapshot()` renders the accessibility tree which may omit content that's conditionally rendered (via React state) but present in the DOM. After clicking interactive elements (FAQ accordions, product tabs, dropdowns), verify with `browser_console(expression="document.querySelector(...).textContent")` to confirm content is actually rendered — not just that the snapshot changed.

## E-commerce Sites

When testing an e-commerce site, load `references/ecommerce-checklist.md` after the main workflow for a task-specific checklist covering search, cart, product detail, navigation, and content flows.

{"action": "write_file", "name": "nextjs-docker-swarm", "file_path": "references/[REDACTED_TOKEN].md", "file_content": "# El Viajero — CartProvider Fix & Deploy ([REDACTED_PHONE])

## Root Cause of Add-to-Cart Not Working

The cart badge and add-to-cart buttons appeared to work (no console errors), but `localStorage.getItem('viajero-cart')` was always empty after clicking "Agregar".

## Diagnosis Steps

1. Checked if CartProvider is in root layout: `grep "CartProvider" app/layout.tsx` — NOT present
2. Searched all CartProvider wrappings: `grep -rn "CartProvider" app/ components/ --include="*.tsx" | grep -v node_modules`
3. Found CartProvider only in individual page wrappers (page.tsx, tienda-content.tsx, checkout/page.tsx)
4. In browser console, after clicking "Agregar": `JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('viajero-cart') || '[]')` → `[]`

## The Fix

**Add CartProvider + ToastProvider to root layout** (`app/layout.tsx`):

```tsx
import { CartProvider } from "@ai-whisperers/commerce/cart/cart-context"
import { ToastProvider } from "@/components/toast"

// In body:
<CurrencyProvider>
  <CartProvider>
    <ToastProvider>
      {children}
    </ToastProvider>
  </CartProvider>
</CurrencyProvider>
```

**Remove CartProvider from individual pages:**
- `app/page.tsx` — removed import and wrapper
- `components/pages/tienda-content.tsx` — removed ToastProvider import, kept CartProvider (tienda wraps itself differently)

## Docker Build with Supabase Build-Args

The Dockerfile uses `ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and `ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` in the builder stage. Failing to pass these causes prerender errors at build time:

```
Error: @supabase/ssr: Your project's URL and API key are required to create a Supabase client!
```

**Build command:**
```bash
docker build \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://qyvokpribmbrosafntqa.supabase.co \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=[REDACTED_SUPABASE_KEY] \
  -t elviajero:prod .
```

## Deploy

```bash
docker service update --force elviajero_web
```

Verify: `curl -sI https://el-viajero.paragu-ai.com | head -5`

## Key Lessons

- CartProvider NOT in layout is the #1 add-to-cart bug in Next.js e-commerce apps. Always check layout first.
- The `dogfood` skill's DOM snapshot limitation note applies: after clicking a button, the snapshot may not show state changes. Check localStorage programmatically instead.
- Docker builds with admin pages that create Supabase clients need build-args even for public-facing prerendered pages.
"}

# Client Sales Pitch

Build a complete, named-prospect sales pitch for a Paraguayan business lead. This skill covers the full process: research the prospect's business, profile the decision-maker, craft the pitch document, and handle follow-up.

## Pipeline

```
Lead identified → Business research → Prospect profiling → Pitch document → Review → Deploy
```

## Phase 1: Business Research

Before writing a single word of the pitch, understand the business deeply:

1. **Read the live site** — browse via browser_navigate, note:
   - Products/catalog size and categories
   - Pricing display (or lack thereof)
   - Brand voice and visuals
   - Existing CTAs (WhatsApp, phone, contact form)
   - About page (history, team, location)

2. **Read the repo documents** — check for:
   - `CLAUDE.md` — agent guide, project overview, team roles
   - `content/es.json` (or equivalent) — all product data, FAQ, pages
   - `docs/` directory — brand guides, client questionnaires, architecture docs
   - `README.md` — project purpose, tech stack

3. **Extract business intelligence** from content:
   - Location (city, neighborhood, street if available)
   - Product count and categories
   - Current sales channels (WhatsApp, local, social media)
   - Any existing online presence (website, Facebook, Instagram)
   - Pricing patterns (fixed prices vs request-quote)

4. **Check the live site via browser** — confirm:
   - Site is working and accessible
   - Product catalog is complete
   - WhatsApp integration is functional
   - Mobile responsiveness (Paraguay is mobile-first)

## Phase 2: Prospect Profiling

Gather information about the person you're pitching to:

- **Name and role:** Owner? Manager? Decision-maker?
- **Relationship to team:** How did this lead come in? (referral, cold outreach, existing contact?)
- **Pain points to address:** What specific problems does their current setup have?
- **Motivation:** Why would they say yes? (growth, modernization, competition, convenience?)
- **Objections to anticipate:** What will they push back on? (price, tech skills, time, trust?)

If the user knows the prospect already, ask directly for:
- Their personality style (direct? analytical? relationship-first?)
- Any previous conversations or context
- Their biggest business concern right now

## Phase 3: Pitch Document Structure

Create the pitch at `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/PITCH_<BUSINESS>_<OWNER>.md`

## Pitch File Location

Pitch documents are saved to `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/PITCH_<BUSINESS>_<OWNER>.md` during creation. But the CLIENT-facing repo (e.g., `elviajero-comercio`) needs a copy in its `docs/` directory so the URL is accessible:

- Source: `/root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/PITCH_*.md`
- Copy to: `/root/<repo>/docs/PITCH_*.md` (same filename)
- Then `git add`, `git commit`, `git push` to the client repo

The user will share the GitHub URL with the prospect, and the URL format is:
`https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/<client-repo>/blob/main/docs/PITCH_*.md`

**Always check:** after creating the pitch, ask yourself "does the user need to share this link?" If yes, copy it to the client repo and push.

## Structure

```markdown
# Pitch de Ventas — [Owner Name] / [Business Name]

*Cliente: [context — e.g., Primer caso de éxito e-commerce de Paragu-AI]*

---

## Perfil del Cliente

**Negocio:** [Description]
**Ubicación:** [City, neighborhood]
**Productos:** [Count + categories]
**WhatsApp:** [Primary sales channel]
**Presencia actual:** [Local + WhatsApp + social media]

---

## Pitch Inicial (WhatsApp)

Short first-contact message. Natural, not salesy. Opens with genuine interest.

---

## Pitch Completo (llamada o presencial)

**Apertura** — Recognize their situation. "El Viajero tiene un local en Mariano Roque Alonso y vende por WhatsApp, ¿cierto?"

**El Dolor** — 3-5 specific pains of their current setup. Concrete, not generic.

**La Solución** — What we built/delivered. How it works step by step.

**El Diferenciador** — Why this is different/better. "La web no reemplaza tu WhatsApp — lo potencia."

**La Oferta** — First-client pricing or special terms. Be specific.

**El Intercambio** — What we ask in return (testimonial, video, referral pipeline).

**Cierre** — Specific call to action with next step.

---

## Objeciones esperadas y respuestas

List 3-5 objections with concrete responses. Common ones:

- "Ya tengo Facebook/Instagram"
- "No sé manejar eso"
- "Cuánto tiempo toma"
- "Y si no funciona"
- "Por qué tan barato los primeros meses?"

Each objection gets: the exact objection + a response that validates + reframes + solves.

---

## URLs a mostrar

List of live URLs to show during the pitch. These are concrete proof.

**WhatsApp demo note:** Show how clicking a product opens WhatsApp with product info preloaded.

---

## Seguimiento

3 scenarios with exact scripts:

- **Si dice que sí:** Next steps, timeline, what to expect
- **Si dice "lo voy a pensar":** Gentle urgency + space to decide
- **Si dice que no:** Graceful exit with door open
```

## Phase 4: First-Client Offer Template

When this is the first e-commerce client (1000+ products), use the confirmed marketing technique:

```
Setup: 0 Gs
3 meses completamente gratis
Mes 4 en adelante: 639.000 Gs/mes (Plan Empresa)

A cambio:
1. Testimonio honesto
2. Video de 30 segundos
3. Caso de estudio (sin costo para ellos)
```

The pitch hook: "Tu tienda online funcionando sin pagar nada hasta el mes 4."

## WhatsApp Output Rules

When presenting the pitch to the user on WhatsApp:
- NO markdown tables
- Plain text lists with clear labels
- Guarani prices first, USD in parentheses
- One scenario/layout at a time, not all options
- Keep descriptions to 1 sentence per item

## Important Session Pattern (May 8, 2026)

**El Viajero case:** The site was already fully built and deployed before the client was contacted. The pitch was not "we can build you a site" but "we already built your site — here's how it works and why you need it."

This changes the pitch dramatically:
- No technical risk for the client ("ya está todo listo")
- The offer shifts from "let's build" to "let's launch"
- The 48-hour hook still works: "en 48 horas está en línea desde hoy"
- The value is about visibility (Google), not about creation

**First-client offer:** When the site is already built as a speculative project, use the first-client marketing technique (3 months free) with exchange terms (testimonial + video + case study).

## Pitfalls

- **Don't write the pitch in English — the user and prospect are Spanish speakers.** Write the pitch document in Spanish.
- **Don't guess business details.** Read the actual content files and live site. Every detail in the pitch must be verifiable.
- **Don't skip the objection section.** The user will use it in live conversation. 3-5 objections minimum.
- **Don't genericize the pitch.** It must feel specific to THIS business, not copy-paste from another pitch.
- **Don't forget the "intercambio" section.** The user needs to know what to ask for in return for the first-client discount.
- **The "48-hour" hook is your strongest card.** Lead with it when presenting time-to-value.
- **When presenting to the user, send the pitch file reference, not the full content in WhatsApp.**
- **The Paragua-IA docs live at /root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/**

## Verification

- [ ] Business researched via live site + repo content
- [ ] Prospect profile collected or asked about
- [ ] Pitch document saved to /root/paragu-ai-leads/docs/05_strategy/
- [ ] All sections present: perfil, approach, pain, solution, differentiator, offer, exchange, close
- [ ] 3-5 objections with specific responses
- [ ] 3 follow-up scenarios
- [ ] First-client offer included if applicable
- [ ] File referenced to user on WhatsApp (not full content pasted)
- [ ] Spanish language throughout
